claim: Obesity is determined solely by environmental factors.

result.claim: Obesity is determined solely by environmental factors.
result.reasoning: The claim that obesity is determined solely by environmental factors is strongly refuted by multiple studies. Evidence consistently highlights a significant genetic component in determining human fatness. An adoption study (Paper ID: 29025270) found a strong correlation between adoptees' weight and their biological parents' body-mass index, but no correlation with their adoptive parents, concluding that genetic influences are important while the family environment alone has no apparent effect. A genetic analysis (Paper ID: 3315558) explicitly states the presence of genetic factors in obesity. Furthermore, a study on twins reared apart (Paper ID: 27393799) estimated substantial genetic influences on body-mass index (heritability of 0.70 for men and 0.66 for women) and concluded that the childhood environment has little or no influence. Familial clustering of obesity has also been observed (Paper ID: 19307912), further supporting a genetic predisposition. While environmental factors can play a role, the evidence clearly indicates that they are not the sole determinants of obesity.

evidence.text: BMI is a strong predictor of overall mortality.
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: The age- and race-adjusted odds ratios of overweight or obesity (body mass index > or =25) ranged from 2.1 to 3.7.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 60-120% higher diabetic mortality (HR 2.16 [1.89-2.46]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Suspected familial obesity was observed in 2.4 percent of random recall group whites.
Disorders of lysine acetylation may be correlated with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
evidence.source: Protein Lysine Acetylated/Deacetylated Enzymes and the Metabolism-Related Diseases

evidence.text: BMI was associated inversely with overall mortality.
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Suspected familial obesity was observed in 6 percent of hyperlipidemic recall group whites.
Genetic influences on body-mass index contribute to the similarity of the body-mass index of twins later in life.
evidence.source: The body-mass index of twins who have been reared apart.

evidence.text: Commensal and pathogenic bacteria can influence obesity.
evidence.source: Amyloid-DNA Composites of Bacterial Biofilms Stimulate Autoimmunity.

evidence.text: Thirty-one percent of top decile Quetelet index white probands from the hyperlipidemic recall group came from families where at least two other first-degree relatives were similarly obese.
The study examined the contributions of genetic factors and the family environment to human fatness.
evidence.source: An adoption study of human obesity.

evidence.text: Nineteen percent of top decile Quetelet index white probands from the random recall group came from families where at least two other first-degree relatives were similarly obese.
The study considered ten established environmental risk factors.
evidence.source: Gene–environment interactions in 7610 women with breast cancer: prospective evidence from the Million Women Study

evidence.text: The childhood environment has little or no influence on the body-mass index of twins later in life.
evidence.source: The body-mass index of twins who have been reared apart.

evidence.text: The analysis of genetic factors in obesity was carried out on nuclear families from Aosta, N. Italy.
evidence.source: Genetic analysis of human obesity in an Italian sample.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 20% higher mortality from all other causes (HR 1.20 [1.16-1.25]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Within-family clustering of hypertension was common in kindreds with suspected familial obesity.
